course_description:
This course provides Dentistry students with an introduction to important concepts in genetics, biological chemistry, and dental tissue engineering that are of particular relevance to future Dentists. It builds on the concepts learned in first year chemistry and applies new concepts to health problems encountered in dental practice.

The 2025 version of DENT2222 has been designed with student feedback incorporated from 2023-2024, to ensure an improved student learning experience.

Specific changes that have been incorporated include

.
course_aims: DENT2222 aims to familiarise Dentistry students with the basics of genetics, biological chemistry, and dental tissue engineering and apply this to oral and systemic diseases and clinical situations. Students will also get an introduction to the tissue engineering used to treat oral disease.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain the clinical importance of altered nutrient intake in a dental context.
Assess the impact of bone metabolism and diseases on care planning for dental treatment.
Illustrate consequences of endocrine disorders on oral health and disease.
Understand the role played by genetic, cellular, and molecular abnormalities on oral cancer and orofacial clefts.
Appreciate specific molecular mechanisms that regulate wound healing, tissue repair and tissue regeneration in dental therapies.
Describe dental tissue engineering and advances in materials and scaffolds.

assessment_details.title: Science Communication Group
assessment_details.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Performance
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: A major focus of this course is for the student to develop a comprehensive understanding of the science underpinning dental practice, especially around molecular process in health and disease as well as the effect of key systemic conditions on oral health. A secondary focus is for the student to develop an appreciation of how specific molecular mechanisms that regulate wound healing co-relate to tissue regeneration in dental therapies, and understanding the rapid advances occurring in this area. As part of this assessment, students will work in teams of 4 students: Students are expected to work cohesively as a team to search, analyse and critique relevant literature with respect to systemic condition/s contributing to oral health and disease, in turn, influencing dental care planning. Students can further incorporate concepts of dental tissue engineering and how this is used to manage specific conditions. The detailed review will inform the scripting and creation of an engaging 10 minute video by the student team. Student teams will need to contribute as per a Couse Team Charter provided at start of semester, and this team charter will need to be submitted as part of the final submission. The assessment will be marked to published criteria standards that will be provided via the learning management system. All three components will need to be submitted by each team before the due date. Students will also rate team member contributions and a peer assessment factor will be applied to the final mark. Detailed task descriptions, marking criteria and expectations will be provided on Blackboard at start of semester. Learning activities will be scaffolded to permit students time to create and build rapport to complete team based learning activities in class. Please note that use of flowcharts, graphics and images should be from UQ licenced sources such as Adobe Stock and all material must be appropriately acknowledged as per the Copyright Act. Note to student teams: You must acknowledge where you used AI in this assessment, including where you have directly quoted or paraphrased AI-generated content or used tools to summarise readings, brainstorm ideas, plan your process, or for editing/ proofreading purposes or for video content creation. You may be asked to acknowledge AI using a cover sheet, form, template or table. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
